coredata.dc:title: Flexible all-solid-state high-performance supercapacitor based on electrochemically synthesized carbon quantum dots/polypyrrole composite electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Jian, Xuan
Yang, Hui-min
Li, Jia-gang
Zhang, Er-hui
Cao, Le-le
Liang, Zhen-hai
coredata.dc:description: Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a new zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial have become a focus in electrochemical energy storage. In this paper, flexible all-solid-state supercapacitors (ASSSs) were electrochemically synthesized by on-step co-deposition of appropriate amounts of pyrrole monomer and CQDs in aqueous solution. The different electrodeposition time plays an important role in controlling morphologies of stainless steel wire meshes (SSWM)-supported CQDs/PPy composite film. The morphologies and compositions of the obtained CQDs/PPy composite electrodes were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Raman spectrum and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Furthermore, a novel flexible ASSS device was fabricated using CQDs/PPy composite as the electrode and separated by polyvinyl alcohol/LiCl gel electrolyte. Benefiting from superior electrochemical properties of CQDs and PPy, the as-prepared CQDs/PPy composite ASSSs exhibit outstanding electrochemical performance with the areal capacitance 315mFcm−2 (corresponding to specific capacitance of 308Fg−1) at a current density of 0.2mAcm−2 and long cycle life with 85.7% capacitance retention after 2 000 cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Polypyrrole
Electrodeposition
Flexible
All-solid-state supercapacitor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of PPy and CQDs/PPy composite
Electrochemical characterization of the flexible all-solid-state supercapacitor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation process for the CQDs/PPy flexible ASSS.

SEM images of the as-prepared electrodes: (A) low-magnification view of the CQDs/PPy mesh with an average pore diameter of about 35μm, the inset shows the original SSWM before electrodepositing of CQDs/PPy; (B) morphology of the CQDs/PPy electrodeposited on SSWM; (C) high-magnification view of the CQDs/PPy mesh with a porous surface; (D) low-magnification view of the pure PPy coated SSWM; (E) and (F) morphology of the pure PPy electrodeposited on the SSWM. TEM images of CQDs/PPy composite (the CQDs were marked with yellow circles) with different magnifications (G-I).
(A): FTIR spectra of CQDs, PPy and CQDs/PPy composite; (B) Raman spectra of PPy and CQDs/PPy composite.

(A) XPS survey spectrum of CQDs/PPy composite, PPy and CQDs; (B) C1s spectra of CQDs; (C) C1s spectra of pure PPy; (D) C1s spectra of CQDs/PPy composite; N1s spectra of PPy (E) and CQDs/PPy composite (F).

(A) Comparison of CV curves of the CQDs/PPy composite electrodes with the increase of deposition time from 10 to 50min (Scan rate: 10mVs−1); (B) Comparison of GCD curves of the CQDs/PPy composite electrodes with different deposition time (from 10 to 50min) at current density of 0.5mAcm−2; (C) Areal capacitance of different CQDs/PPy electrodes at various scan rates (10∼200mVs−1); (D) Comparison of CV curves of CQDs/PPy-30 and PPy-30 electrodes at a scan rate of 10mVs−1; (E) Comparison of GCD curves of CQDs/PPy-30 and PPy-30 electrodes at a current density of 0.5mAcm−2; (F) Nyquist plots of CQDs/PPy-30 and PPy-30 electrodes.

Electrochemical performance of the symmetric supercapacitor. (A) Cyclic voltammogram curves at different scan rates; (B) Galvanostatic charge/discharge curves from 0.2 to 4mAcm−2; (C) Areal capacitance of the pure PPy and CQDs/PPy supercapacitors at various current densities; (D) Nyqusit plots of the pure PPy and CQDs/PPy supercapacitors; (E) Plots of the Cre and Cim of the specific capacitance vs. frequency; (F) Cycle stability at 2mAcm−2, inset showing the GCD curves of the first and the last five cycles.
Ragone plots of as-assembled CQDs/PPy supercapacitor device: on the basis of the geometric area.

Electrochemical performance of assembled CQDs/PPy all-solid-state supercapacitor under different being angles of 0°, 45°, 90°, 135° and 180°. The inset of CV curves at 50mVs−1 and a digital image of the flexible device.
(A) and (B) are the optical digital photographs show a red LED lighted powered by three as-made CQDs/PPy composite supercapacitor devices connected in series.
Comparison of active material mass loading, areal capacitance and specific capacitance of CQDs/PPy composite electrodes with various electrodeposition times.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Both Ca and Cs are obtained from CV curves at a scan rate of 10mVs−1.

head.ce:title: Flexible all-solid-state high-performance supercapacitor based on electrochemically synthesized carbon quantum dots/polypyrrole composite electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan, Shanxi, 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a new zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial have become a focus in electrochemical energy storage. In this paper, flexible all-solid-state supercapacitors (ASSSs) were electrochemically synthesized by on-step co-deposition of appropriate amounts of pyrrole monomer and CQDs in aqueous solution. The different electrodeposition time plays an important role in controlling morphologies of stainless steel wire meshes (SSWM)-supported CQDs/PPy composite film. The morphologies and compositions of the obtained CQDs/PPy composite electrodes were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Raman spectrum and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Furthermore, a novel flexible ASSS device was fabricated using CQDs/PPy composite as the electrode and separated by polyvinyl alcohol/LiCl gel electrolyte. Benefiting from superior electrochemical properties of CQDs and PPy, the as-prepared CQDs/PPy composite ASSSs exhibit outstanding electrochemical performance with the areal capacitance 315mFcm−2 (corresponding to specific capacitance of 308Fg−1) at a current density of 0.2mAcm−2 and long cycle life with 85.7% capacitance retention after 2 000 cycles.

ce:section.ce:para: Along with the unceasing technical progress, the flexible electronic device has become an essential equipment in each family. Flexible all-solid-state supercapacitors (ASSSs) as one kind of most important flexible electronic device have attracted lots of attention due to their high power delivery ability, flexibility, easy fabrication, good safety, convenience and long cycling life [1–4]. Currently, in order to achieve a more excellent electrochemical performance supercapacitor, many transition metal oxides (such as MnO2 [5], Ni(OH)2 [6] and Co3O4 [7]) and carbon nanomaterials (such as graphene [8], carbon nanotube [9] and carbon nanofibers [10]) are used for electrode material of supercapacitor. However, owing to relatively poor cycle-life for transition metal oxides electrode and comparatively lower capacitance for carbon-based nanomaterials electrode, which restrict their application in electrochemical energy storage.

In the recent years, numerous of conducting polymers (such as polyaniline [11], polypyrrole [12] and polythiophene [13]) have been extensively explored in supercapacitors due to their large storage capacity and cost-effective processability. In particularly, the widely use of polypyrrole (PPy) is predominant because of its easy synthesis, environment stability and good electrical conductivity [14]. However, poor flexibility and cycle stability are difficult problems, which restrict the use of PPy in flexible electronic device. Therefore, to overcome these disadvantages, the preparations of PPy/carbon-based nanomaterial composite have been developed. Such as Wang’ group [15] designed a novel flexible ASSS based carbon fiber paper/polypyrrole electrode. Tsai’ group [16] and Kim’ group [17] both have reported a direct electrochemical method to synthesize graphene/PPy composite electrode for supercapacitor. These previous researches have well demonstrated that carbon-doped PPy composite can significantly reinforce the electrochemical performance of supercapacitor.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a novel zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial have been attracted extensive attentions, particularly with their focus on a promising material for energy storage. Owing to their high surface area, more active edge sites, good electrochemical conductivity and well dispersed in various solvents, CQDs can considerably improve the electrochemical performance of supercapacitor [18–21]. Such as, Zhu et al. reported a new application of CQDs/RuO2 network as an excellent electrode material for supercapacitor [22]; Wei et al. prepared CQDs/NiCo2O4 nanocomposite applied to high-performance supercapacitor electrode [23]; Bhattacharya et al. synthesized a novel hybrid of Fe3O4 nanospheres and CQDs for superior electrochemical energy storage performance [24]; Xie et al. reported CQDs-PPy/TiO2 nanotube hybrid as a flexible ASSS electrode material for energy storage [25].

It is well known that direct electropolymerization of PPy onto the substrate is the best way to fabricate different nanostructure [26]. So in the present work, a flexible ASSS, using stainless steel wire meshes coated with CQDs/PPy composite as electrode, is prepared by an efficient and controllable potentiostatic electropolymerization method (without added electrolyte). Due to the advantages of both graphite and quantum dots, the CQDs can play a supporting electrolyte role during the electropolymerization process. The pyrrole monomeric radical cations (Py•+) would gradually polymerized on surface of the CQDs to form the porous structure of CQDs/PPy composite. The supercapacitor properties of CQDs/PPy composite electrodes were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement. According to these results, the as-fabricated flexible ASSSs based on CQDs/PPy composite exhibits more superior electrochemical performance compared with pure PPy, indicating it has a great potential for energy storage device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PPy and CQDs/PPy composite
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CQDs and CQDs/PPy composite were taken with a Tecnai G220 (FEI, USA) operating at 200kV. The surface scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of CQDs/PPy composite morphology were investigated with a JSM-7001F (JEOL, Japan). The CQDs/PPy composite structural information was determined from a Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR-Prestige-21, Shimadzu Corporation, Japan) using a standard KBr pellet method. Raman spectrum was taken with laser confocal micro-Raman spectroscopy (LabRAM HR800, France). Elemental compositions of the samples were detected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo, ESCALAB 250Xi, USA).

Pyrrole (Py, A.R.), Lithium chloride (LiCl, A.R.) and Lithium perchlorate anhydrous (LiClO4, A.R.) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, average M n ≈1750) was purchased from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Stainless steel wire meshes (SSWM) were purchased from Hebei Anping Ouxin Harware Product Co. Ltd. (Hebei, China). All other chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Double distilled ultrapure water (DI water) with an electric resistance >18.3MΩ was used for all solution preparation.

The as-produced water soluble CQDs were synthesized through direct electrochemical approach according to the previous report [27]. Briefly, two graphitic rods were set as anode and cathode at 30V potential with continuous electrolysis for 120hours. Then the dark yellow solution was centrifuged to remove the precipitate (the TEM of CQDs can be seen Fig. S1).

Prior to electrochemical deposition, the SSWM (mesh numbers of 400) with a size of 1.5×2cm2 was carefully polished using emery paper, then was ultrasonically washed with DI water, 0.5M H2SO4 and ethanol for several minutes to remove impurities and dried at room temperature. The CQDs/PPy composite film was electrodeposited on the SSMW at+0.8V in a mixture solution containing 0.2M pyrrole monomer and 0.4mgmL−1 CQDs. After deposition, the CQDs/PPy composite electrode was rinsed with DI water for several times to remove unreacted chemicals and dried at room temperature. For the purpose of comparison, the CQDs/PPy composite films were electrodeposited on the SSMW for different time (10–50min), named as CQDs/PPy-10, CQDs/PPy-20, CQDs/PPy-30, CQDs/PPy-40, and CQDs/PPy-50, respectively. The relationship of the electrodeposition time with the mass loadings of pure PPy and CQDs/PPy composite films are listed in Table 1 . The pure PPy film was deposited on the SSMW at same conditions in an aqueous solution containing 0.2M pyrrole and 0.25M LiClO4.

The flexible ASSS was assembled by two CQDs/PPy composite electrodes with a piece of filter paper as separator, and PVA-LiCl aqueous gel was used as electrolyte. The PVA-LiCl gel electrolyte was prepared as follows: 50mL of DI water was heated to 85°C, and then the 4g of PVA and 3.2g LiCl was slowly added with vigorous stirring until the mixture solution was clear. At last, the PVA-LiCl gel electrolyte was carefully smeared on the two CQDs/PPy composite electrodes and dried at room temperature for an hour. The two CQDs/PPy composite electrodes were pressed together under a pressure to fabricate the flexible ASSS.

All electrochemical experiments in this work were performed using a V3 electrochemical workstation (Princeton, USA). A three-electrode system, including a Ag/AgCl reference electrode (RE), a Pt foil was applied as the counter electrode (CE) and CQDs/PPy modified stainless steel wire meshes were used as the working electrode (WE) in an electrolyte of 1.0M KCl. In the two-electrode system, the capacitor cells were assembled by using two pieces of all-solid-state CQDs/PPy composite electrodes as the cathode and anode, respectively. These were separated by PVA-LiCl gel electrolyte. Cyclic voltammogram (CV) measurement were performed at the various scan rates in the potential range of 0∼0.8V. The galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) was conducted at different current densities. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out in the frequency range of 0.01–100 000Hz with an AC amplitude of 5mV.

In three-electrode system, the areal capacitance (CA ) was evaluated by CV curve using the equation below:
(1)
C A
=
∫
i d V
2 S ×
Δ
V × v
( F
cm
− 2
)
Where i was the discharge current (A), ΔV was the scan potential window of the CV curve (V), S was the surface area of the electrode material (cm2) and v was the scan rate (V s−1).

In two-electrode system, the specific capacitance (Cs ) was measured by GCD method using the equation below:
(2)
C s
=
2 × i ×
Δ
t
Δ
V × S
(
F
cm
− 2
)
Where i was the discharge current (A), Δt was the discharge time (s), ΔV was the potential window of the discharging (V), S was the surface area of the electrode material (cm2).

The energy density (E) and power density (P) of a supercapacitor device could be calculated according to the following Eqs. (3) and (4), respectively:
(3)
E =
C ×
Δ
V
2
7200
( W h c
m
− 2
)
(4)
P =
3600 E
t
( W c
m
− 2
)
Herein, C was the total capacitances (Fcm−2), t the discharge time (s) and ΔV the cell voltage (V).

As shown in Fig. 1 , CQDs/PPy composite was deposited on the surface of stainless steel wire meshes (SSMW) as flexible electrode via electrochemical polymerization strategy has been investigated. In this process, SSMW and CQDs/PPy composite film were respectively employed as the current collector and electroactive material. The image clearly shows that the difference of surface morphology of SSMW before and after electropolymerization. In addition, the CQDs/PPy composite film exhibits larger roughness compared with naked SSMW, indicating the substrate was coated with more electroactive material. Based upon above analysis, we can predict the preparation of CODs/PPy flexible ASSSs by electrochemical polymerization method have many advantages: (1) The SSMW as current collector has many superior properties, such as excellent flexibility, good conductivity and large surface area; (2) The rough nano-structure surface of CQDs/PPy composite film would be benefited to the moving and transport electrolyte ions. All these merits guarantee a good electrochemical performance of the CQDs/PPy composite electrode.

Fig. 2 shows the SEM and TEM of the pure PPy and CQDs/PPy composite films deposited at a constant potential of 0.8V for 30min. As shown in the inset of Fig. 2(A), the original SSWM before coated by CQDs/PPy (or PPy) is knitted by smooth steel wires with an average pore diameter of approximately 35μm. From the micrographs of Fig. 2(A–C) and (D–F), one can obviously see the surface morphology of two polymer films have distinctly different structures. The CQDs/PPy composite film has a porous structure (Fig. 2(C)), whereas the pure PPy demonstrates the formation of dense film (Fig. 2(F)). To further characterize the inner structure and formation mechanism of the CQDs/PPy composite, the TEM was performed. As shown in Fig. 2(G) and (H), a large number of CQDs were embedded in the inner structure of PPy to form the CQDs/PPy composite. In particularly, the image of Fig. 2(I) exhibits the lattice fringes with an interplanar distance of 0.32 and 0.313nm, which are corresponding well to the (002) spacing of graphitic carbon [27]. Based on the above characterization of SEM and TEM analysis, the growth of CQDs/PPy composite can be attributed to the strong electrostatic absorb interaction between CQDs and pyrrole monomer. It is possible that the CQDs acted as nucleation sites due to abundant oxygen groups (such as hydroxyl and carboxyl) on the edge of CQDs, under the action of electrostatic attraction, the pyrrole monomeric radical cations (Py•+) would gradually polymerized on surface of the CQDs to form the “nano-islands” structure of CQDs/PPy hybrid. Therefore, such “nano-islands” structure not only greatly increase an effective interface area to improve the charge transfer, but also facilitate the fast transfer of charge carriers during the charge-discharge process [23,28].

Furthermore, we have also investigated the impact of deposition time on the surface morphology of CQDs/PPy composite films. Fig. S2 displays representative optical image of CQDs and PPy is co-electrodeposited on the SSWM for different periods from 10 to 50min. The CQDs/PPy composite films appear darker with increase of deposition time which suggests the different deposition time has an obvious effect on the morphology of the CQDs/PPy composite films. As shown in Fig. S3, the surface of the SSMWs are coated by CQDs/PPy composite films, and the thickness of the SSMW is increase when the electrodeposition time goes from 10 to 50min (Fig. S3 (B), (E), (H), (K) and (N)). Meanwhile, the micrographs of Fig. S3 (C), (F), (I), (L) and (O) demonstrate that the CQDs/PPy composite films become denser with the increase of electrodeposition time. In conclusion, the different electrodeposition time has an obvious effect on the surface morphology of CQDs/PPy composite film, and the “nano-islands” structure of CQDs/PPy would gradually grow with the increase of electrodeposition time.

For the structure measurement, the FTIR and Raman spectra of the CQDs, PPy and CQDs/PPy composite are shown in Fig. 3 , respectively. As shown in Fig. 3(A), for CQDs, four characteristic peaks located at 1725, 1444, 1243 and 865cm−1 corresponding to CO, CC, COC and aromatic Ar-H are observed, which is attributed to the oxygen-contaning group on the CQDs edge [27]. As respected, the characteristic peaks appear in the spectra of PPy and CQDs/PPy composite at 1546, 1455, 1308, 1043 and 919cm−1 are assigned to CC, CN stretching vibration, CN in-plane deformation, CH deformation vibration, CC in-plane bending vibration in the pyrrole ring, respectively [26]. This indicates that the PPy and CQDs/PPy successfully deposited onto the surface of SSMW via electrochemical polymerization method. It should be noted that the peaks located at 1725cm−2 in CQDs spectra and 1703cm−2 in CQDs/PPy composite spectra are found. This appearance of the peak for CQDs and CQDs/PPy composite correspond to CO groups, indicating that the CQDs was successfully doped into PPy. The CO stretching vibration in CQDs/PPy composite was shifted to 1703cm−2 (from 1725cm−2) due to the π-π interaction and hydrogen bonding interaction between CQDs and pyrrole rings in polymer [17,28]. To further investigate the inner structure of PPy and CQDs/PPy, Raman spectra analysis is shown in Fig. 3(B). For pure PPy Raman spectra, the characteristic bonds peaking at 927, 1044, 1356 and 1572cm−1 are obsevred, which are attributed to the bipolaron ring deformation, C-H bending vibration, the ring stretching mode and C-C backbone stretching [28,29]. When CQDs doped into PPy, the CQDs/PPy composite exhibits two prominent bonds at 1531 and 1555cm−1, it suggested that the CQDs/PPy was successfully obtained. This is in good agreement with above FTIR results.

Next, the X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) measurements were carried out to evaluate the chemical environment of CQDs, PPy and CQDs/PPy composite films. As seen in Fig. 4 (A), the XPS survey spectrum of CQDs, PPy and CQDs/PPy are displayed three major peaks, revealing the main elements on the as-prepared electrode material are C, N and O. The detailed chemical state of CQDs was characterized by deconvolution of C1s spectra. The presence of components peaks (289, 286.8, 284.8 and 285.7eV) in Fig. 4(B) indicates the CQDs have abundant oxygen groups, such as hydroxyl, carbonyl and carboxylic acid groups on its edges [30]. It is noticed that the existence of the characteristic peak of Cl2p at a binding energy (BE) of 208eV indicates PPy was successfully electropolymerized in the LiClO4 electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 4(C), three major peaks located at 284.5, 285.4 and 288.4eV, which can be assigned to sp2 carbon, C-N and CO, respectively. In Fig. 4 (D), four component peaks located at 284.7, 285.6, 286.7 and 288.9eV are attributed to sp2 hybridized carbon, C-N, CO and COOH, which indicate the C1s core level XPS spectrum of CQDs/PPy composite film combines the characteristics of both CQDs and the pure PPy [29,31].

Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 4(E), the major peak component at a BE of 399.8eV is assigned to N-H, the high BE tail (401eV) which originates from the positively charged nitrogen (-N+) and the low BE component at 398eV which can be assigned to N= [32]. For N1s XPS spectrum of CQDs/PPy-NW composite, Fig. 4(F) showed three components peaks (400.8, 399.7 and 397.5eV), which are attributed to −N+, NH and N, respectively. Such difference might be the CQDs have abundant of oxygen groups on its edges, which can acted as active nucleation sites and was incorporated into pyrrole monomer to balance the positive charges during the electropolymerization procedure. This result can be well consisting with the SEM, TEM and FTIR characterization analysis.

The electrochemical capacitance of CQDs/PPy composite electrodes were evaluated in a three-electrode cell 1.0M KCl electrolyte, with a Pt foil and an Ag/AgCl as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 (A), the CV integral area of CQDs/PPy composite electrodes increases with increasing deposition time from 10 to 50min, indicating a long electropolymerization time is favorable to enhance capacitance. It is worth noting that the CV curves of CQDs/PPy composite electrodes with long deposition time deviate slightly from rectangular which indicates that pseudocapacitance properties is remarkable [16]. Meanwhile, the GCD curves of CQDs/PPy composite electrodes at different electrodeposition time exhibit nearly symmetric linear charge-discharge curves with small iR drops in Fig. 5(B), revealing the CQDs/PPy composite electrodes have good electrochemical capacitance performance. As shown in Fig. 5(C), the rate performance of the CQDs/PPy composite electrodes with different electrodeposition time measured by increment of the scan rates (10∼200mVs−1). Although the CQDs/PPy-50 composite electrode possesses the highest areal capacitance of 516 mF cm−2 at a scan rate of 10mVs−1, a quick drop in capacitance has been observed with the CQDs/PPy-50 composite electrode, and the areal capacitance of CQDs/PPy-40 and CQDs/PPy-50 at a scan rate of 100mVs−1 is 144.2 and 141 mF cm−2, respectively, which are smaller than the CQDs/PPy-30 at the same scan rate (162.4mFcm−2). Furthermore, Table 1 summarized the active material mass loadings and specific capacitance of CQDs/PPy composite electrodes with various electrodeposition times. It can be found that the specific capacitance of CQDs/PPy composite electrodes decreases with the increase electrodeposition time from 30 to 50min. These results can be attributed to the following reasons: (1) the higher capacitance of the CQDs/PPy composite electrodes at long electrodeposition time is due to the more electroactive materials on the SSWM substrate (see SEM images of Fig. S3); (2) at relatively lower scan rate, the ions have enough time to diffuse into the CQDs/PPy composite, the electrode demonstrate higher areal capacitance; (3) with more CQDs/PPy electrodeposited on the surface of SSMW, the thicker film would increase the ions diffusion resistance and electron transport resistance, resulting in quicker drop of capacitance with the increase of scan rates [29,31].

Besides, we have further investigated the difference of electrochemical performance of PPy and CQDs/PPy composite electrode at same electrodeposition time. As shown in Fig. 5(D), compared with pure PPy-30, the CV curve of CQDs/PPy-30 exhibited larger area, mirror-image symmetry and a quasi-rectanglar shape. It is found that the GCD curve of CQDs/PPy-30 composite electrode has lower iR drop and more symmetric shape compared with the PPy-30 at a current density of 0.5mAcm−2 in Fig. 5(E). These results indicate that the CQDs/PPy composite combine two types of energy storage, one is electric double layer capacitance behavior and another is pseudocapacitance [16]. In addition, the high capacitance performance also attributed to the CQDs doped PPy for highly porous “nano-island” structure, which may improve the redox activity of PPy and reduce the ion/electron transport internal resistance to less energy waste.

Electrochemical impedance spectrometry (EIS) as a powerful method can provide the information between electrode and electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 5(F), the Nyquist plots of PPy-30 and CQDs/PPy-30 composite electrode are presented. In contrast, the pure PPy electrode has large semicircle in high frequency region indicating that PPy has large charge-transfer resistance (R ct). In addition, smaller Warburg resistance (R w) of CQDs/PPy in the low frequency region has reflecting that less obstruction for ion diffusion and more ideal supercapacitor capacitive performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the flexible all-solid-state supercapacitor
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, the Nyqusit plots of impedance were collected in the frequency range from 0.01 to 100 000Hz for PPy and CQDs/PPy composite supercapacitor are shown in Fig. 6(D). In comparison to PPy supercapacitor, the plots of CQDs/PPy composite exhibited smaller diffusion impendence and faster charge-transfer rate. Moreover, the Cre and Cim of specific capacitance as function of frequency of CQDs/PPy supercapacitor was shown in Fig. 6(E). The Cim curve presented a sharp peak at a particular frequency (f 0 =0.12Hz), defining the shorter relaxation time of τ 0 =1/f 0 =8.3s. This time constant indicated that the minimum time of CQDs/PPy needed to discharge all the energy from the device with an efficiency of greater than 50% [34–36].

Additionally, the cycle stability of the electrode materials is an imperative parameter to evaluate their potential for practical application. Fig. 6(F) demonstrated long-term cyclic life stability, as tested by GCD charge/discharge for CQDs/PPy composite supercapacitor at a current density of 2mAcm−2. After 2 000 continuous cycles, the as-assembled device displays approximately 85.7% retention of its initial specific capacitance, providing an outstanding cycling performance for CQDs/PPy flexible all-solid-state symmetric supercapacitor. This value was high compared with other PPy-based nanomaterials involving PPy-MnO2@C/TiO2 (84% retention after 1 000 continuous, Ref. [37]), pure PPy film (85% retention after 1 000 continuous, Ref. [38]) and NPG-PPy (78% retention after 900 continuous, Ref. [39]).

More significantly, from the Ragone plots shown in Fig. 7 , it is evident that the as-assembled CQDs/PPy supercapacitor device in this work has a higher energy density (ED) and power density (PD) than that of other reported supercapacitor, including GO/PPy (3.15μWhcm−2 at 5763μWcm−2, Ref. [29]), PPy-GO/CNTs (11.1μWhcm−2, 2.5mWcm−2, Ref. [33]), PEDOT-GO/CNT (2.05μWhcm−2, 2.5mWcm−2, Ref. [33]), MWCNTs@PEDOT/PSS (14.2μWhcm−2 at 99.7μWcm−2, Ref. [40]), PEDOT/SDS-GO (4.88μWhcm−2, 7027μWcm−2, Ref. [41]) and PPy/Carbon paper (2.05μWhcm−2, 482.1μWcm−2, Ref. [42]).

In order to evaluate the potential application of the all-solid-state CQDs/PPy supercapacitor for flexible energy storage under real conditions, the device was pressed with various mechanical bending angles from 0° to 180° and its electrochemical performance was employed by CV at a scan rate of 50mVs−1 (Fig. 8 ). From the CV curves with different curvatures, it can be observed that they are almost displayed the similar rectangular shape even at a large bending angle of 180°, revealing excellent electrochemical capacitance performance reliability under the bending tests. Therefore, this high flexibility property can be attributed to combination of flexible CQDs/PPy composite material electrode and gel electrolyte.

At last, in order to further evaluate the potential practical application of the ASSSs, we have assembled three units of CQDs/PPy flexible device (0.8V each) connected in series were use to power a red light LED (2.4V) in Fig. 9 . Interestingly, the ASSSs could power the red LED for nearly 5min when the charging time less than 1min, confirming their practical and commercial application in flexible energy storage (detail can be seen in the supporting video). Above all, all these superior electrochemical behaviors demonstrated that the flexible CQDs/PPy composite ASSSs have excellent electrochemical performance.

In summary, a superior all-solid-state flexible supercapacitor based on CQDs/PPy composite electrode material can be successfully prepared via electrochemical deposition method. The as-made CQDs/PPy composite electrode displays excellent electrocapacitive properties which can be attributed to the synergistic effect of CQDs and PPy. The flexible ASSSs exhibit good capacitive performance with a high specific capacitance of 315mFcm−2 (corresponding to specific capacitance of 308Fg−1) at a current density of 0.2mAcm−2 and a long life cycling stability of 2 000 continuous cycles. Furthermore, the as-assembled supercapacitor device possesses a good flexibility under various mechanical bending angles from 0° to 180° and practical application. In short, these impressive results suggest that electrochemical synthesized CQDs/PPy composite as electrode materials for supercapacitor is a new approach for the development of flexible electronic device.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is jointly funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China and Shenhua Group Corp. (Grant nos. U1261103), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province of China. (Grant No. 201601D011023), Graduate Science and Technology Innovation Fund Project of Shanxi. (No. 2016BY050) and the Open Fund of State Key Laboratory of Coal Conversion, Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science (No. J12-13-913). The authors thank for anonymous reviewers for their helpful suggestions on the quality improvement of our present paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2017.01.082.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
